feedback.text: I would like to add that this initiative has the ability to tackle another major problem in digital fairness, and it would be wonderful to see it addressed here. Over the years, an increasing number of video games have started to rely on online services to operate. Hundreds to thousands of developers spend years laboring to create a video game, and millions of players both spend time on it and purchase either the video game itself or elements of it, only for it all to cease existing when it's deemed unprofitable. This is destruction of art, history, and in almost all cases a destruction of property that the consumers paid for. It doesn't have to be this way, though. It is possible to leave video games that rely on online services playable after their end of service. As of July 31, a European Citizens' Initiative by the name of Stop Destroying Videogames is reaching its deadline. This initiative has had such a massive reach and been supported so strongly by consumers that it more than met the signature goal, with almost 1,500,000 signatures at the time of writing. Consumers' concern over video games not being destroyed is real. Many statistics exist out there proving the video game industry is the most profitable of them all, many times more than films and music. People care about it deeply and want to see it protected. Again, it would be lovely to see this initiative address this problem. If needed, there are many professionals in the field with the expertise to guide efforts to make it an easier transition, too. The people behind the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative would be great partners to nail down the specifics.
